extra.message: User-controlled 'name' parameter is interpolated into an f-string on line 38, then passed to Template().render() on line 41. This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). An attacker can supply payloads like {{7*7}} or {{config.__class__.__init__.__globals__}} to achieve expression evaluation and potentially remote code execution via Jinja2 sandbox escape.

User-controlled 'name' parameter is directly embedded into HTML via f-string on line 38. Since the interpolation happens before Jinja2 template processing, the name value becomes literal HTML in the template source. Jinja2 auto-escaping only applies to {{ }} expressions, not static template text. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the name query parameter (e.g., /?name=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>), enabling cross-site scripting.

Hardcoded secret value 'John Ripper' is assigned to SUPER_SECRET_NAME instead of being loaded from an environment variable. The FIXME comment on the same line acknowledges this should use os.getenv(). Hardcoded secrets in source code can be exposed via version control, CI/CD logs, or repository access.

Hardcoded secret token '5u93R53Cr3tT0k3n' is assigned to SUPER_SECRET_TOKEN instead of being loaded from an environment variable. The FIXME comment on the same line acknowledges this should use os.getenv(). Hardcoded credentials in source code are a security risk if the repository is compromised.

FastAPI debug mode is set to config.DEBUG. When DEBUG is enabled (e.g., via DEBUG=True in .env_temp), detailed error pages and stack traces are returned to the client on unhandled exceptions. This can leak sensitive information about the application internals, file paths, environment variables, and source code.

The application makes an HTTP GET request to a URL controlled by the PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL environment variable on every request to the root endpoint. While not directly user-controlled, if an attacker gains the ability to control environment variables (e.g., via a separate vulnerability or CI/CD compromise), this could be leveraged for Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) against internal services. The response body from the external service is also directly embedded into the HTML output without sanitization.

The 'name' query parameter on the root endpoint accepts any user input with no validation, sanitization, or length restrictions. It flows directly into an f-string that is rendered as a Jinja2 template and returned as HTML. This is the entry point for both the SSTI and XSS vulnerabilities on lines 38-41. Additionally, there is no rate limiting on this endpoint, allowing an attacker to abuse the public IP lookup service or perform denial-of-service attack by triggering outbound HTTP requests on every call.
